coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-functionalized metal−organic framework for the dual detection of tetracycline and norfloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xinyi
Hong, Dilong
Zhang, Di
Jiang, Yuliang
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline and norfloxacin are two important antibiotics known for their effective treatment of inflammation. However, their excessive use inevitably leads to serious environmental hazards. Rapid and highly sensitive detection of their trace amounts is crucial for early prevention of related diseases. While there are numerous reported fluorescent probes capable of highly sensitive detection of tetracycline and norfloxacin individually, very few can simultaneously detect both. Developing a probe capable of detecting both antibiotics simultaneously is particularly necessary to enhance detection efficiency. In this study, carbon quantum dots derived from fragrans were functionalized with Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) materials. This successful approach enabled simultaneous detection of both tetracycline and norfloxacin, with detection limits of 0.15 μM and 0.87 μM, respectively. Furthermore, successful detections were achieved in water, milk and soil samples, yielding excellent results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline
Norfloxacin
Fluorescent probes
Carbon quantum dots
Metal-Organic Framework

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of TC and NFX
Fluorescence detection of TC and NFX in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The (A) SEM, (B) TEM and (C) TEM-Mapping images of CDs@UiO-66-NH2. .
The (A) PXRD and (C) BET diagrams of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2. (B) FT-IR and (D) the potential diagrams of UiO-66-NH2, CDs, and CDs@UiO-66-NH2.
Fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2, CDs and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (Inset: UiO-66-NH2 (a) and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (b) under ultraviolet light.).

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 at various (A) TC and (C) NFX concentrations. (B) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and different TC concentrations. The inset illustrates the linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and TC within the range of 0 μM to 33 μM. (D) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of NFX. The inset displays the linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and NFX within the range of 0 μM to 65 μM.
PXRD spectra of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 after immersion in solutions of varying pH.
(A) PXRD graph of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2+TC/NFX, (B) Zeta potential graph.
Determination of TC in actual samples (n = 3).
Determination of TC in soil samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-functionalized metal−organic framework for the dual detection of tetracycline and norfloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, Jiangsu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline and norfloxacin are two important antibiotics known for their effective treatment of inflammation. However, their excessive use inevitably leads to serious environmental hazards. Rapid and highly sensitive detection of their trace amounts is crucial for early prevention of related diseases. While there are numerous reported fluorescent probes capable of highly sensitive detection of tetracycline and norfloxacin individually, very few can simultaneously detect both. Developing a probe capable of detecting both antibiotics simultaneously is particularly necessary to enhance detection efficiency. In this study, carbon quantum dots derived from fragrans were functionalized with Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) materials. This successful approach enabled simultaneous detection of both tetracycline and norfloxacin, with detection limits of 0.15 μM and 0.87 μM, respectively. Furthermore, successful detections were achieved in water, milk and soil samples, yielding excellent results.
Herein, a novel CDs@MOF was prepared and demonstrated rapid and highly sensitive responses to tetracycline and norfloxacin which was further applied in water, milk, and soil successfully.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) and norfloxacin (NFC) are both commonly used antibiotics in medicine [1–3]. Tetracycline is widely employed in treating various bacterial infections and diseases, such as respiratory, urinary tract, skin infections, and parasitic diseases, exhibiting notable efficacy [4–8]. Norfloxacin, on the other hand, is a broad-spectrum antibiotic belonging to the fluoroquinolone class. It is primarily used to treat urinary tract infections, such as UTIs, and infections caused by bacteria in the intestines, among others [9–15]. Both types of antibiotics have made significant contributions to disease treatments. However, with the rise in antibiotic resistance, reliance on these medications has increased. This may lead to some pathogenic microorganisms losing sensitivity to antibiotics, escalating the difficulty in treating infections. Of particular importance, during production, residues of antibiotics may be discharged into water bodies through wastewater or seep into the soil [16–20]. Alternatively, they may enter the environment through human and animal excreta. This affects aquatic and soil microorganisms, potentially impacting the entire food chain within the environment. Thus, limiting the discharge of antibiotics into the environment is crucial. However, even more critical is conducting monitoring and assessment of trace amounts of tetracycline and norfloxacin to prevent irreversible environmental consequences caused by these antibiotics.

Traditional high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography, and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [21–23] have played pivotal roles in detecting these two types of antibiotics. However, their intricate and time-consuming procedures have become inadequate to meet the current demand for rapid detection. The development of fluorescence analysis has addressed these issues, propelling antibiotic detection into a new era. In recent years, numerous reports on fluorescence probes for TC and NFC have emerged, showcasing significant progress in rapid and high-sensitivity detection [24–30]. Nevertheless, most of the currently reported probes can only individually detect tetracycline or Norfloxacin, which does not align with the trend toward multifunctional probes. The design of a probe capable of simultaneously detecting both antibiotics would not only aid in cost-saving during preparation but also align with the developmental trend of probe technology [31–33].

Carbon quantum dots, as high-performance carbon nanomaterials, find extensive applications in sensing, energy storage, photovoltaics, and other fields [34–38]. In probe research, using bio- based materials as precursors to fabricate carbon dots instead of traditional toxic organic molecules and inorganic quantum dot probes exemplifies the principles of green chemistry. In this study, carbon quantum dots derived from fragrans were prepared and functionalized into MOF materials, successfully achieving rapid simultaneous detection of tetracycline and Norfloxacin (<1 s), demonstrating high sensitivity (0.15 μM for TC, 0.87 μM for NFX). Furthermore, the practical applicability of this probe was validated in water, milk, and soil samples.

2-Aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC), triethylenetetramine (TEDA), florfenicol, and neomycin (NEO) were acquired from Adamas-beta® (Shanghai, China). Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4) was obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl), KCl, MnCl2, AgNO3, NiCl2, Co(NO3)2, PbCl2, and CrCl3 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Methionine (Met), tryptophan (Trp), threonine (Thr), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), and leucine (Leu) were sourced from Huixing Biochemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Amoxicillin (AMX), norfloxacin (NFX), tetracycline (TC), chloramphenicol (CPL), erythromycin (Er), and streptomycin (SM) were obtained from Macklin. All chemical reagents were used directly without further purification.

The sample morphology was observed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Thermo Fisher, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using JEM-210. F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was utilized for fluorescence spectral measurements. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted with ALPHA II (Bruker, Germany). Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy was performed using a Cary 50 spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). The elemental composition was analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher, USA). The material structure was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan). Zeta potential was determined using Zetasizer Ultra (Malvern Panalytical, Britain). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurements were obtained with an ASAP 2460 instrument (Micromeritics, USA).

The preparation method of UiO-66-NH2 refers to literature [39]. Firstly, 1.08 mmol ZrCl4 was added into the mixed solution containing 10 mL DMF and 2 mL HCl with stirring for 5 min, and sonicated for 30 min. After adding 20 mL DMF containing 1.5 mmol NH2-BDC to the above solution and stirred for 30 min. The above mixed solution was added to the Teflon reactor for reaction. After cooled to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm and washed with DMF for five times. Then, the material was soaked in methanol for three days and replaced the methanol every 24 h. Finally, the product was centrifuged and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven.

After cleaning the fruits from the osmanthus tree, separated the flesh from the core. Then, grind the pulp and added a certain amount of TEDA (pulp: TEDA=7:3). The mixture is evenly transferred to the reactor, heated at 180 °C for 5 h, cooled to room temperature, and the products was filtered with 0.22 um filter head for reserve use.
The original CDs solution was diluted ten times and added into UiO-66-NH2 (0.2 g) for 1 h ultrasound, standing for 12 h, and the product was obtained after centrifugation and drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TC and NFX
ce:section.ce:para: 0.01 g of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 sample was dispersed into 100 mL ultrapure water and the suspension was obtained by ultrasound for 30 min. Then, adding different concentrations of TC and NFX to suspension for fluorescence experiments. Then, fluorescence experiments were carried out by gradually adding different concentrations TC/NFX solution to the suspension of CDs@UiO-66-NH2. After both are fully reacted, the suspension was determined with a 328 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TC and NFX in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of TC and NFX in real samples were detected by standard addition method. The soil samples were taken from different places at NJNU. The samples were dried in an oven for 12 h and acidified with aqua regia for 6 h. Then, samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for three times and filtered with 0.22 μm for later use. The tap water was taken from the laboratory, industrial wastewater was acquired in Kukdo Chemical Co., Ltd and the milk used in the test was purchased from the school supermarket. The milk sample was pretreated, a certain amount of acetonitrile was added to remove the protein in the milk, and the supernatant was diluted with a 0.22 um filter for later use. Different concentrations of TC and NFX were added to milk samples to test the fluorescence emission spectra under 328 nm excitation. Each group of data was measured three times in parallel.

As shown in Fig. S1, the average diameter of CDs is 2.04 nm, and the SAED pattern of CDs has a clear diffraction ring. By comparing simulated peaks, we confirmed the successful preparation of UiO-66-NH2 ( Fig.S2) .The structures and morphologies of synthesized UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 were investigated using SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 and S3, UiO-66-NH2 displayed a spherical shape with an average particle size of approximately 210 nm. Upon CDs modification, there was no significant change observed in the morphology of the MOF. To verify whether the modification by CDs affected the structure of UiO-66-NH2, the crystal structure properties of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 were further analyzed via PXRD. From Fig. 2
A, diffraction peaks at 7.4° and 8.5° corresponded to the (111) and (200) crystal planes of Zr-MOF [39], respectively, and the diffraction peaks of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 aligned with these peaks, indicating that the framework remained unaffected after modification. Infrared spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 were tested (Fig. 2 B). The peak at 481.5 cm−1 corresponded to Zr-O in UiO-66-NH2, while the peak at 1568.2 cm−1 belonged to C = O. After the introduction of CDs, the characteristic peaks of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 remained unchanged, suggesting that CDs might have been embedded into the pores of UiO-66-NH2. As observed in Fig. S4, a new peak appeared at 328 nm for CDs@UiO-66-NH2, indicating the generation of new substances. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 at 77 K are shown in Fig. 2 C. The surface area (BET) of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was 16.3977 m2/g, significantly smaller than that of UiO-66-NH2 (193.1826 m2/g), further confirming the successful incorporation of CDs into the cavities of UiO-66-NH2. Elemental composition analysis of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was further performed using XPS. Spectra at 532.27, 399.15, 286.17, and 183.27 eV corresponded to O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, and Zr 3d, respectively (Fig. S5A). According to the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S5B), peaks at 287.70, 285.51, and 284.17 eV corresponded to OC = O, CN, and C = C, respectively. From the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S5C), peaks at 532.18, 530.81, and 529.45 eV were attributed to CO, Zr-O, and C = O, respectively. Further analysis of the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S5D) revealed peaks at 400.57 and 398.75 eV, corresponding to NH and CN, respectively. Zeta potential measurements of CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 were conducted (Fig. 2 D), indicating an electrostatically driven combination between CDs and UiO-66-NH2.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the introduction of CDs resulted in a significant enhancement of fluorescence, further confirming the successful synthesis of CDs@UiO-66-NH2. To test the selectivity of CDs@UiO-66-NH2, we selected a series of substances at equal concentrations and measured the changes in fluorescence intensity after their addition (including KCl, MnCl2, ZnCl2, AgNO3, NiCl2, Co(NO3)2, Met, Trp, Thr, His, Cys, Leu, AMX, Florfenicol, Er, SM, CPL, NEO, NFX, TC). As depicted in Fig. S6 the fluorescence intensity noticeably diminished upon the addition of TC and NFX, while the impact of other substances on fluorescence intensity was minor. The experimental results suggest that CDs@UiO-66-NH2 can be utilized for detecting TC and NFX. Fig. S7 illustrates changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 in the presence of various metal ions, amino acids, and antibiotics as interfering substances. From the results in the figure, it can be observed that potential interfering substances have minimal impact on the determination of TC and NFX.

In order to test the response time of the probe to the detected substance, the following experiments were conducted. From Fig. S8 it's evident that upon addition of certain concentration TC/NFX solution into the suspension of CDs@UiO-66-NH2, the fluorescence intensity quickly quenches over time, reaches stability. As depicted in the fluorescence titration curves (Fig. 4
A), at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 consistently decreases as the TC concentration increases from 0 μM to 230 μM. Illustrated in Fig. 4 B, within the TC concentration range of 0 μM to 33 μM, the data exhibits a good linear relationship. The linear regression equation is represented as F = −99.681C + 8103.9 (where C is the TC concentration), with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9983. The method's lowest detectable limit (3σ/k) is calculated to be 0.15 μM. We also comparison of different MOF probes for detecting TC in Table S1. The fluorescence titration curve (Fig. 4 C) demonstrates a gradual decrease in fluorescence with increasing NFX concentration from 0 μM to 300 μM at room temperature. For NFX concentrations within the range of 0 to 65 μM, the linear regression equation is expressed as F = −47.55C + 7916.6 (where C is the NFX concentration), with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9968. The method's lowest detectable limit (3σ/k) determined from this data is 0.87 μM. To confirm that it is CDs@UiO-66-NH2 detecting TC/NFX rather than CDs, we conducted further experiments. As shown in Figure S9, when the CDs solution was diluted and an equal amount of TC/NFX was added, no color change was observed, indicating that the CDs solution cannot be used to detect the two antibiotics.

To confirm the stability of the material, the relevant experiments were conducted under diverse conditions. Powdered samples were submerged in solutions with varying pH levels for 10 days, after which PXRD testing was performed (Fig. 5 ). The diffraction peaks exhibited no changes. Additional assessments involved immersing the powder in ultrapure water for different periods, followed by PXRD and fluorescence intensity measurements as depicted in Fig. S10A. The structural integrity of the material remained intact, with only minimal fluctuations observed in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S10B). Taken together, these experiments collectively demonstrate the exceptional stability of CDs@UiO-66-NH2.

To better understand the detection mechanism of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 towards TC/NFX, PXRD, XPS, Zeta potential, and UV–vis tests were conducted. After the addition of TC/NFX, the PXRD peak patterns remained unchanged, indicating that fluorescence quenching was not due to structural collapse (Fig. 6
A). The UV absorption peaks of TC/NFX largely overlapped with the excitation spectra of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S12), suggesting that the fluorescence quenching mechanism might be attributed to the Inner Filter Effect. Upon the addition of TC/NFX, the surface charge of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 changed from 12.7 mV to −9.8 mV/−7.16 mV, indicating electrostatic interactions leading to fluorescence quenching (Fig. 6 B).

To validate the practicality of CDs@UiO-66-NH2, its probe was employed for the feasibility testing of actual samples using the standard addition method to determine the recovery rates. Following a series of sample preparations, a certain concentration of TC was introduced into the sample, and the fluorescence intensity was measured. The experimental results, as shown in Table 1 and Table 2
, indicate that the recovery rates for TC ranged between 84.23% and 113.26%, with a relative standard deviation below 1.83%. Employing the same approach for detecting NFX in milk, soil and water (refer to Table S2, Table S3 and Table S4), the recovery rates fell within the range of 88.70% to 117.18%, with an RSD below 0.74%. These experimental findings collectively demonstrate the applicability of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 for detecting TC/NFX in real samples.

Briefly, carbon quantum dots were prepared using osmanthus fragrans flowers and functionalized into a MOF, resulting in a post-modified MOF-based fluorescent probe named CDs@UiO-66-NH2. The structural characterization of this probe was conducted via UV spectroscopy, SEM, TEM, XRD, and XPS. Fluorescence detection exhibited rapid and highly sensitive responses for both TC and norfloxacin NFX, representing significant progress compared to traditional probes capable of detecting only one antibiotic. To assess its performance in real environments, the probe was employed for detection in various water samples, milk, and soil preparations. The outcomes underscored the excellent detection capabilities of CDs@UiO-66-NH2, aligning with the trends in green chemistry and multifunctional probe development.
Xinyi Liu: Data curation. Dilong Hong: Formal analysis. Di Zhang: Investigation. Yuliang Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.138567.
